author: @darrenstyle4283
text: Hey R.M-S I own a 3d printer and it has a heated bed. The problem is that there is not an even distribution of heat with the current design. If you did 30 minutes of research (youtube, type in Creality CR-10) and I think you could make a better heatbed and you could make a lot of money. If you want to send me one 30cm x 30cm, I'd love to test out my theory.

author: @dremaboy777
text:
Ok... now that I'm on this band wagon,  and you got my brain completely doped on graphene, research and experimentation.  Realizing that the holy-grail is "surface area", I ran into MOFs !!!
(metal-organic framework). Holy hindu cow!!!  
Can you make any of these? teach us to make one? or make one that we can buy from you?

or is the utensils for making such things too out of reach; and the amazing carbonized sugar surface area is our biggest football field for the moment?

your life force and generosity ceases to amaze me. you say you just supply that nuts and bolts.  but, is YOUR nuts and bolts that's changing and shaping the future. no matter the intricacy of the machine; without nuts n bolts... it's just strap metal. 

The stick: when I write the wind blows it away. 
The dust: I'm to light to hold it - persay.
Robert: YOU NEED A BINDER !


LOLOLOL

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Lol - awesome mate - I will look into MFs and a home made version for you - but probably after the indiegogo is done - i am a bit tied up and busy with that right now - cheers

replies.author: @dremaboy777
replies.text: I would soooooo appreciate that.  not just me, all of us. yes, I know you really really busy. but I can wait. Also can't wait till you make this historic drive.  words fall short of describing my excitement; for the drive, and all that you give us to be interested in regarding the creativity and potential of the human and his mind.

author: @jamest.5001
text:
using it sinking heat in to the wood? nice DC cook top!  I need one of these! off grid cooking.  I could use a DC buck converter to adjust and drop from my 25-29v average bank voltage. I have a question! again! has anyone thought about using something like this on solar panels. to deice them . on winter. I guess a 20-30 deg. C. would be plenty. we don't get a lot of snow n ice here. when we do it sucks. two days ago we had 3" of sleet . it would be easier to heat and melt . than a building a tilting mount. so the ice don't build up.
a a liquid circulation system could work double duty. heating when iced. and cooling when its 100+ out in summer. I know  I know! I'm dreaming. but I have seen panels drop nearly 2v as they were heated by the sun. that could be a lot on smaller systems in hot locations.  and on the battery ramble . I'm just going to go to the store for now. buy nos  lithium cells. build a Tesla style power wall in a trunk.  I'm over 100ah at 29.4v peak. now. that's going to be like one cell in the Bank. when I have enough battery to have time. I plan on experiment on building cells. of some kind. start with shot glass sized NiFe batteries. the life is why I'm interested in those. 
my LG cells are coming in at 100-150 ma over spec on average. I found a high drain Samsung cell. got warm while charging I think it showed 1/2 ohm resistance.  and 500mah. it almost discharged as fast as a cap. it started out OK. I guess something happened while charging. it charged over 1.5 hours @ .5a then . then testing at .5 amp discharge. it went fast. and was well under 600ma. so I tested again the same results . and it was warmer than the cells beside it. the load resistors can warm them some. but not what heated this cell. and the internal resistance. is 100 times the others. well crap. did it again sorry I'm rambling. again!  I guess I mostly ramble and it helps me think. sorry. I would like to have a low power DC cook top. I was thinking induction cook top. I even was thinking maybe I could build one. not sure of power required. you gotta pay close attention when you running off batteries. anyway great video. hey. you gonna eat that egg?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: defo but not as it would have done on the glass top it tends to be 12-14 C in the lab - looks like Marcus has the same idea mate and you ramble away mate lol

author: @JamesWilson-pq9qp
text: This could be a real life saver in blizzard conditions. A few solar panels and some batteries and you could stop the pipes from freezing.  I believe the design limit is about 60ºC or 140ºF so boiling water is not possible.  How about oil pipe lines, this would keep the black gold flowing.

replies.author: @everest3334
replies.text:
60ºC or 140ºF in a partial vacuum will boil water no problem, see roberts other related videos.

The boiling point of water is 100°C, 212°F or 373.15 Kelvin under standard conditions at sea level (at one atmosphere of pressure).

replies.author: @everest3334
replies.text: OC if  you have the power from A few solar panels and some batteries then producing and collecting 2 hydrogen/ 1 oxygen gases electrolosis, 12v 2 amps is good enough, would provide you more localised heat etc in that case , you just need to produce a small portable device to  make it on site and on the go.

replies.author: @JamesWilson-pq9qp
replies.text: Be careful with hydrogen. Believe me it is not fun to play with. You don't want it your backyard. Oxygen is not to much fun either. Suddenly you'll have fires you can't see, surprise, poof!  And also so far the catalysts are very inefficient and use expensive rare metals. We need to store energy safely!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the limit of this tend to be about 65 degrees C for heating apps mate

replies.author: @JamesWilson-pq9qp
replies.text: Nice job Rob, inspirational!  Most electric blankets are 24 volts, much safer than 220.

author: @RWBHere

replies.author: @everest3334
replies.text:
Various melting points of different types of plastic: 

The melting point of HDPE (High Density Polyethelyne) is about 130 ºC 
The melting point of LDPE (Low Density Polyethelyne) is about 110 ºC 
The melting point of PET (Polyethylene terphthalate) is about 250—260 ºC 
The melting point of PP (Polypropylene) is about 160—170 ºC 
The melting point of PS (Polystyrene) is about 70—115 ºC 
The melting point of PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride) is about 75—90 ºC

author: @richardteychenne3950
text:
Rob a whole range of useful portable camping applications come to mind. A roll up hot dog heater, something to stop her indoors from complaining of cold sleeping bags and a thermal alarm clock for hard to move teenagers. The list is endless.
On a serious note there might be a valuable product as a portable warming device for mountain rescue situations if coupled with a good carbon blade battery, now where can one find one of those!
Also I have some free time although I am probably too far away to pop over and help daily on the Twizy project if you need some extra resource I would be glad to help where I can.
FWG making life a positive experience.
